name: 1944 Cuba–Florida Hurricane
summary: Major hurricane of October 1944. Peak intensity 125 kt (Cat 4) over western Cuba with minimum pressure 937 mb. Crossed the Dry Tortugas as Cat 3 on Oct 18, then made mainland Florida landfall near Sarasota on Oct 19 as a strong Cat 2 (90 kt, 962 mb). Exited the NE Florida coast as a tropical storm before becoming extratropical over the Carolinas.

archives.note: First Florida landfall, Oct 18 at 9:00 PM local time; Category 3 (90 kt, 949 mb).

photos.title: Dry Tortugas Light on Florida Reefs
photos.description: Original caption: A beacon to mariners, warning of the treacherous Florida reefs at the entrance to the Gulf of Mexico, the Coast Guard lighthouse on Loggerhead Key has withstood storms and wars for 118 years. It is officially known as Dry
photos.source: National Archives at College Park - Still Pictures (via DPLA)

archives.note: Southernmost major city; extensive damage documented by Monroe County Public Library.

archives.note: Second (mainland) landfall near Sarasota, Oct 19 ~7:00 AM local time; Category 2 (90 kt, 962 mb).
Storm surge of 3–6 ft inundated the entire island; 15+ cottages destroyed; fishing pier lost.

photos.title: Ruth Brame in front of the Gulf Shores Inn
photos.source: State Archives of Florida / Florida Memory

archives.note: Exit region; 43 homes damaged on north end of Fletcher Ave; storm became extratropical over the Carolinas.

archives.note: Storm track crossed central Florida on Oct 19, 1944, near Orlando after Sarasota landfall.
Storm passed just south and exited the NE Florida coast near Jacksonville/Fernandina on Oct 19, 1944.

news.title: The Southern Jewish Weekly — issue late due to the hurricane which struck Jacksonville Thursday
news.snippet: "Sorry We are Late! This issue of The Southern Jewish Monthly is late due to the hurricane which struck Jacksonville Thursday. — The Editor." (Oct 19, 1944 was a Thursday — masthead-dated Oct 1 but published post-storm.)
news.source: The Southern Jewish Weekly (Jacksonville) — LOC Chronicling America

news.title: Gadsden County Times — tropical hurricane struck St. Petersburg, Tampa, Jacksonville (160 mph)
news.snippet: "...struck the St. Petersburg and Tampa area this morning and was turning to the northeast. It was expected to reach Jacksonville sometime in the late afternoon... the hurricane was said to have reached a velocity of 160 miles per hour... traveling forward at about 12 miles per hour."
news.source: Gadsden County Times (Quincy) — LOC Chronicling America

archives.note: Historic coastal city lashed by the storm's NE quadrant on Oct 19–20, 1944; widespread flooding and downed trees.

archives.note: Collier County coast north of the Cape Romano / Ten Thousand Islands; pier destroyed and shoreline peat exposed after the Oct 19, 1944 hurricane.

photos.title: Remains of the Naples beach pier after the hurricane
photos.source: State Archives of Florida / Florida Memory

photos.title: Mangrove peat exposed after the hurricane
photos.description: Geology—Florida—Collier County; shoreline peat exposed by storm erosion.
photos.source: State Archives of Florida / Florida Memory

archives.note: East-coast metro ~150 mi east of the Gulf-side storm path; felt outer rain bands and lost citrus/fruit crops.

news.title: Miami Citizen — fruit blown from trees during recent hurricane
news.snippet: "...the secretary of agriculture in the state of Florida be requested to assist the fruit growers in salvaging the fruit which was blown from the trees during the recent hurricane..."

archives.note: Hernando County, ~45 mi N of Tampa; the storm centered just west and caused 25-90% vegetable losses and major citrus damage across the region.

news.title: Brooksville Journal — citrus growers biggest sufferers from tropical hurricane
news.snippet: "Hernando county citrus growers will be the biggest sufferers from the tropical hurricane which visited the state and this section last night and this morning at an early hour... damage, however, to citrus can be considered slight in comparison with other sections where the storm proved to be the worst."
news.source: The Brooksville Journal — LOC Chronicling America

news.title: Brooksville Journal — vegetable losses 25-90%, OPA asked to remove ceilings
news.snippet: "On the basis of Florida vegetable losses ranging from 25 to 90 percent due to the hurricane, the Florida Vegetable Committee has requested that all vegetable price ceilings be removed by the Office of Price Administration..." Also: power-company line crews were brought in from as far as Atlanta and Birmingham.
news.source: The Brooksville Journal — LOC Chronicling America
